items.question: Who reports RMSE and MAE in a machine learning project?
items.answer: ML engineers, data scientists, and analysts report these metrics to stakeholders, in model cards, and on leaderboards after evaluating held-out validation or test predictions — not training-set fits.

items.question: What are RMSE and MAE?
items.answer: RMSE = √(average squared residual (y − ŷ)²); MAE = average |y − ŷ|. Both summarize regression prediction error on a scored dataset in the same units as target y.

items.question: When should you compute RMSE and MAE?
items.answer: After training, on validation data for model or hyperparameter selection, and on test data for final unbiased performance estimates — never on the same rows used to fit model parameters without a proper split.

items.question: Where do RMSE and MAE appear in the ML workflow?
items.answer: On the evaluation branch after predict on held-out splits: notebooks, CI model checks, experiment trackers, and production monitoring dashboards comparing current vs baseline regression quality.

items.question: Why use RMSE instead of raw MSE for reporting?
items.answer: RMSE restores linear units of y (dollars, °C) by taking the square root of MSE, making the number interpretable as a typical error magnitude while still penalizing large residuals more than MAE does.

items.question: How does RMSE relate to MSE training loss?
items.answer: RMSE = √MSE on the same residuals. Training minimizes MSE (Loss Functions topic); evaluation reports RMSE = √(test MSE). Rankings are identical; only scale changes. MAE uses the same formula for training loss and test metric.

items.question: RMSE vs MAE — how do they treat large errors?
items.answer: RMSE squares residuals before averaging and taking the square root, so one error of 10 contributes more than two errors of 5. MAE averages |residual| linearly — each unit of error counts equally. RMSE ≥ MAE always.

items.question: Evaluation metric vs training loss — RMSE/MAE vs MSE/MAE?
items.answer: Formulas match (MSE/MAE in Loss Functions). Training loss is minimized during fit on train batches. RMSE/MAE here are reported on held-out val/test to estimate generalization — same math, different data split and purpose.

items.question: RMSE vs mean baseline — when is a model useful?
items.answer: A model beats the mean baseline when its test RMSE is lower than predicting ȳ for every row. If test RMSE exceeds the standard deviation of test y (roughly, for centered errors), the model may be no better than a constant predictor.

items.question: Cause: A few test examples have very large |y − ŷ|. Effect on RMSE vs MAE?
items.answer: RMSE rises sharply because squared large residuals dominate MSE. MAE increases proportionally to the outlier count and magnitude but is less explosively pulled — the RMSE–MAE gap widens, signaling heavy tails.

items.question: Cause: Data leakage lets test features include target information. Effect on reported RMSE?
items.answer: Test RMSE and MAE look artificially low — the model appears strong in evaluation but fails on truly unseen production data because metrics were computed on contaminated splits.
